title: Craft Paper Roll Holder
summary: A tiny wall or table stand that dispenses a roll of craft paper on a dowel, with a gap underneath to tear off a sheet. Eight pocket screws and a dowel — done in an hour.
description:
This is a minimalist craft-paper dispenser: two little 1x2 uprights holding a 1/4" dowel that a roll of kids' craft paper spins on, with the roll feeding down and out under a bottom board you tear the paper against. It's about the simplest useful thing you can build — a length of 1x2, one dowel, and eight pocket screws — and it's done in under an hour.

The build is two end assemblies joined by a bottom board. Each end is an L or U of 1x2 with a hole drilled for the dowel; a 3/8" spade bit gives the 1/4" dowel a little room to spin freely. Pocket screws (set for 3/4" stock) make clean hidden joints, or countersunk 2-1/2" screws work if you don't have a jig.

The one dimension that matters is the gap under the bottom board: leave 3/4" of clearance so the paper can pass under and feed out for tearing. Too tight and the paper jams; the gap is the whole function. Slide the dowel through the roll and into the end holes and it's ready — no glue on the dowel so you can reload. Mount it on a wall or leave it freestanding on a craft table. A great quick gift for a kid who draws.

tools.note: For the hidden joints; countersunk screws also work.
Or a miter/hand saw — all short cuts.
A 3/8" spade bit for the dowel holes gives the 1/4" dowel room to spin.

materials.name: Wood dowel, 1/4 in, 24 in
materials.note: Roll spindle — no glue so it's reloadable.

materials.name: Wood glue
materials.note: For the frame joints, not the dowel.

cutList.note: Dowel hole drilled with a 3/8" spade bit.

steps.title: Build the two ends
steps.body: Build each end assembly with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, or 2-1/2" screws and a countersink bit. Drill the dowel hole in each upright with a 3/8" spade bit — the extra room over the 1/4" dowel lets the roll spin freely.

steps.title: Join the ends with the bottom board
steps.body: Join the two ends with the bottom board, leaving a 3/4" gap under it so the paper can pass under and feed out for tearing. That gap is the whole point — too tight and the paper jams.

steps.title: Insert the dowel and finish
steps.body: Slide the dowel through the paper roll and into the end holes — no glue, so you can lift it out to reload. Sand and finish if you like. Mount it to a wall or leave it standing on the craft table.

images.alt: Small wooden craft paper roll holder with a dowel spindle
